serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of stir-fried sliced beef vermicelli weighing approximately 350 g, as typically served in a Singapore Vietnamese-style restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice Vermicelli with Beef (estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef stir-fry and rice noodle components)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried sliced beef vermicelli found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by component analysis: cooked rice vermicelli (~55% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #20066), lean stir-fried beef slices (~30% by weight, USDA FDC #174032 beef round stir-fry), vegetables and sauces (~15%). Weighted average per 100 g calculated. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of this dish.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI from International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 derived from rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low GI, per Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283 and University of Sydney GI Database). The overall dish GI is moderated by the protein content of beef and the presence of vegetables, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 x 18.5 x 3.5) / 100 = 34.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 1820 mg sodium, which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving delivers approximately 64.8 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 34.2; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and monitoring portion size is advisable to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content (~1820 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~4.6 g per serving); choosing lean beef cuts and requesting less sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk associated with this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Sliced Beef Vermicelli (干炒牛肉米粉) is a Vietnamese-influenced noodle dish popular in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant scene. It consists of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bún) stir-fried at high heat in a wok together with tender slices of marinated beef, bean sprouts, spring onions, garlic, and a seasoning blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly smoky wok-hei flavour, a tender-chewy noodle texture, and the umami richness of the beef. It is typically served as a main course and is a staple offering at Vietnamese-style eateries in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 518 kcal, 32 g protein, 14 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (~53) due to the rice vermicelli base, but carries a high sodium load (~1820 mg per serving) from the sauces used. Clinically, it is of concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to sodium content, and requires carbohydrate monitoring for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding lactose, but contains soy and gluten-containing sauces.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods data for cooked rice vermicelli and lean stir-fried beef. GI is based on the analogous rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods) as no exact dish-level entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or other primary regional sources. Sodium value of 520 mg per 100 g reflects typical soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning for this dish style and is consistent with published values for similar stir-fried noodle dishes. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef and the halal certification status of the specific restaurant (Viet'Spice) is not confirmed in the source data. GI of 53 is classified as low per the Veritas threshold rule (GI <= 55 = low). Glycemic load per serving = (53 x 18.5 x 3.5) / 100 = 34.2.
